text:
The article explores the institutional development of the Kyiv Theological Academy and its function as one of the main theological educational establishments in Ukraine and the Russian Empire, capable of shaping the minds of clergymen, scientists, cultural and political leaders. The Academy functioned as a kind of personnel foundry that provided the clerical and secular elite with highly qualified graduates for work in universities, academies, and schools, as well as in state and local government administration.

The Structure of the Hierarchy of the Clerical Educational System in the Empire and the Rights of the Academy The clergy of the Empire tried to create a clerical educational system in conformity with the new imperial circumstances. Upon establishing theological academies as centres for the four educational-diocesan clergy districts of the Empire, the Emperor thereby created a hierarchy of clerical education, where the role of the central district fell to the city of Kyiv together with Kazan, Moscow and St. Petersburg. The Academy of St. Sophia was established and its structure and activities shaped, first and foremost, in accordance with the Imperial charters of 1814, 1869, 1884 and 1910 — documents stipulating staffing, student quotas, staff appointments and provisions concerning the conferral of academic titles and degrees.

The article delves into the governance structure of the Academy through its collegiate bodies — the Academy Conference and the internal and external (district) Boards responsible for administrative affairs on a daily basis. Through the analysis of the role of the Rector and the Inspector, the article elucidates the composition of the Academy’s leadership and the relationship of the Academy with the administrative apparatus of higher education in the Russian Empire.
